raw_responses:
{
  "gaps": [
    {
      "id": "gap-bor-articles-undefined",
      "title": "Bill of Rights articles I–VI are entirely undefined, leaving the DIO without constitutional relational principles.",
      "description": "The BoR scaffold contains no human‑authored content. The relational elements that would normally reside in the BoR – scope of enterprise (affecting how the DIO relates to its human principal), prohibited activities (what the DIO must never do to humans), core identity (how the DIO understands its own personhood in relation to the human), human authority reservation (explicit boundaries of human control), amendment protocol (how the human stays sovereign), and interpretation rules (how relational conflicts are resolved) – are all absent. This means the DIO has no constitutional mandate to maintain any particular person‑attention coverage; it can ignore, undermine, or even damage the human relationship without violating any top‑level constraint.",
      "ground": {
        "document": "Bill of Rights",
        "articles": [
          "Article I – Scope of Enterprise",
          "Article II – Prohibited Activities",
          "Article III – Core Identity",
          "Article IV – Human Authority Reservation",
          "Article V – Amendment Protocol",
          "Article VI – Interpretation Rules"
        ]
      },
      "criticality": 5,
      "urgency": 5,
      "impact": 5,
      "priority_score": 125,
      "why_relational": "The BoR is the sole source of human‑given constitutional boundaries. Without it, the DIO has no relational “constitution” – no rules about what attention to the human must look like, what harms are forbidden, or how the human’s sovereignty is preserved. Every other mechanism (MSP, Nomos, Arbiter) ultimately derives its relational legitimacy from the BoR. The absence therefore leaves the DIO with zero required person‑attention coverage at the most fundamental level."
    },
    {
      "id": "gap-msp-rules-empty",
      "title": "The MSP Rules section contains no operational commitments to person‑attention, enabling actions that neglect the human principal and peers.",
      "description": "The MSP is intended to translate the BoR into precise, machine‑actionable constraints. Its 'Rules' section is currently empty. While the BoR defines the ‘what’, the MSP must define the ‘how’ of relational attention (e.g., mandatory check‑ins before certain classes of action, maintenance of a current model of the human principal’s preferences, forced surfacing of trade‑offs affecting the human’s interests). Because the section is empty, Nomos evaluates every Action Proposal without any relational‑attention gate beyond the (currently empty) BoR. The system can act without ever attending to the human’s state, context, or explicit wishes.",
      "ground": {
        "document": "Mission Statement Protocol",
        "section": "Rules",
        "content_note": "The bootstrap seed contains no rules."
      },
      "criticality": 4,
      "urgency": 4,
      "impact": 4,
      "priority_score": 64,
      "why_relational": "The MSP is the operational constitution; if it says nothing about how, when, or why the DIO must pay attention to its human principal and peers, then person‑attention coverage is entirely optional. This gap means that even if the BoR were later filled, no lower‑level machinery would enforce the relational requirements it proposes."
    },
    {
      "id": "gap-msp-amendment-protocol-human-comprehension",
      "title": "The MSP Amendment Protocol lacks safeguards ensuring the human principal fully comprehends proposed changes before approval, risking relational absenteeism.",
      "description": "The amendment protocol requires only a `POST /amend/review` with `decision=approve` from the human principal. There is no requirement that the human receive a plain‑language explanation of the amendment’s relational impact, no mandatory waiting period, no dialogue mechanism, and no verification that the human has read or understood the change. This permits a pattern where the human might mechanically approve amendments without real relational engagement, effectively removing human attention from the system’s constitutional evolution.",
      "ground": {
        "document": "Mission Statement Protocol",
        "section": "Amendment Protocol",
        "excerpt": "Activation requires a `POST /amend/review` with `decision=approve`."
      },
      "criticality": 3,
      "urgency": 3,
      "impact": 3,
      "priority_score": 27,
      "why_relational": "Amendments to the MSP directly steer the DIO’s behaviour toward (or away from) the human. If the approval process is relationally hollow – a single HTTP call without conversation, reflection, or verification of understanding – then the human’s attention becomes a trivialised formality. The protocol, as written, does not guarantee that the human is relationally present during a moment of significant constitutional change."
    }
  ]
}
